docs.title: LEAF DSS Documentation
docs.text: LEAF (Landscape Evaluation & Assessment Framework) Decision Support System is a geospatial platform built by IWMI for evaluating the feasibility of agricultural interventions across blocks and Gram Panchayats in Assam, India. It combines spatial data, multi-criteria analysis, and AI-powered recommendations to help planners identify where specific interventions are most viable.

docs.title: What LEAF DSS Does
docs.text:
LEAF DSS serves four core functions:

  • Geospatial Visualization — Interactive Leaflet maps displaying block-level and GP-level agricultural, water, infrastructure, livestock, and demographic data across all districts in Assam. Overlays include district boundaries and Protected Areas of India.
  • Multi-Criteria Feasibility Scoring — Weighted scoring engine that evaluates each block/GP against configurable variable thresholds (ranges, weights, AND/OR logic) to produce feasibility classes from "Very Low" to "Very High".
  • AI-Powered Recommendations — RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline that combines block-specific metrics with policy documents (PDFs) to generate actionable, citation-backed recommendations via OpenAI.
  • Data Export & Exploration — Download filtered datasets as CSV, explore variable statistics, compare blocks side-by-side, and drill down from district to block to GP level.

docs.title: Data Hierarchy
docs.text:
LEAF DSS organizes data at two administrative levels:

Level Scope Coverage Features Block Administrative blocks (~152) All 27 districts in Assam Full variable coverage: agriculture, water, infrastructure, livestock, collectives Gram Panchayat Village-level clusters (~95) Tinsukia district Detailed livestock density, transport, finance, utilities, activities

When a user selects Tinsukia district, the interface automatically enables GP-level drill-down with a separate set of finer-grained indicators.

docs.title: Map Overlays
docs.text:
The map supports multiple overlay layers:

Layer Description Default Feasibility Choropleth Color-coded blocks/GPs by feasibility score Always on District Boundaries Dashed outlines of all Assam districts Always on Protected Areas National parks, wildlife sanctuaries, conservation reserves across India Off (toggle via checkbox)

docs.title: Intervention Configuration
docs.text: Each intervention (Organic Farming, Micro-Irrigation, etc.) comes pre-configured with relevant variables, acceptable ranges, weights, and preference directions (higher/lower/moderate). Users can customize these in the Configure modal before running feasibility analysis.

docs.title: Variable Choropleth Toggle
docs.text: Click any variable label in the detail view to toggle a single-variable choropleth on the map, letting you visually explore how one indicator varies across blocks.

docs.title: URL-Based Navigation
docs.text: The app supports deep-linking: /<district>/<block>/<gp> URLs let you share or bookmark specific views. The map state reconstructs from the URL on page load.

docs.title: AI Recommendations
docs.text: Click the robot icon on a block detail view to get a GPT-generated recommendation backed by policy document citations. The RAG pipeline uses ChromaDB for vector similarity search over uploaded PDFs.

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Layer Technology Backend Python 3.10+, Flask, GeoPandas, Pandas Frontend Leaflet.js, Chart.js, Bootstrap Icons, vanilla JS Geospatial Shapefiles (Fiona/GDAL), GeoJSON, EPSG:4326 AI/RAG LangChain, ChromaDB, OpenAI GPT API Docs Flasgger (Swagger/OpenAPI), MkDocs Material Deployment Render (Gunicorn), GitHub

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks you through installing LEAF DSS locally, understanding the project layout, and making your first API calls.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+ (3.11 recommended)
  • pip (included with Python)
  • Git for cloning the repository

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone <repo-url>
cd IWMI_LEAF

# Install Flask app dependencies
cd leaf_flask
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Run the development server
python app.py

The app starts at http://localhost:5000 with debug mode enabled. Hot-reload is active — code changes auto-restart the server.

MkDocs Documentation

To also serve the documentation site at /documentation/: 

# From the project root (not leaf_flask/)
pip install mkdocs-material
mkdocs build          # generates site/ directory
python leaf_flask/app.py   # docs now served at /documentation/

docs.title: Key URLs
docs.text: URL Description http://localhost:5000/ Main dashboard — interactive map with feasibility analysis http://localhost:5000/docs Swagger UI — interactive API explorer http://localhost:5000/documentation/ MkDocs documentation site (this site) http://localhost:5000/api API endpoint listing (JSON) http://localhost:5000/health Health check endpoint

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
IWMI_LEAF/
├── leaf_flask/                 # Flask application
│   ├── app.py                  # All API routes + page routes
│   ├── config.py               # Colors, variable groups, map config
│   ├── data_utils.py           # Shapefile/CSV loading, GeoJSON conversion
│   ├── feasibility.py          # Feasibility score calculations
│   ├── rag_utils.py            # AI/RAG recommendation engine
│   ├── requirements.txt        # Python dependencies
│   ├── templates/
│   │   └── index.html          # Main dashboard template (Jinja2)
│   ├── static/
│   │   ├── css/style.css       # All application styles
│   │   ├── js/app.js           # Frontend logic (map, filters, charts)
│   │   └── images/             # Logos (IWMI, CGIAR, GIZ)
│   └── data/                   # Data files (not in git)
│       ├── 4DSS_VAR_2.0.shp    # Block-level shapefile
│       ├── Block_assam.shp     # District-block mapping
│       ├── grampanchayat.shp   # GP-level shapefile (Tinsukia)
│       ├── districts.geojson   # District boundaries
│       ├── protected_areas/    # Protected Areas of India shapefile
│       ├── DSS_input2.csv      # Variable metadata & intervention config
│       ├── Block_GPs.xlsx      # GP-to-block mapping
│       └── Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx  # GP indicator data
├── ai-docs/                    # PDF policy documents for RAG
├── docs/                       # MkDocs source files
├── site/                       # MkDocs built output (generated)
├── mkdocs.yml                  # MkDocs configuration
└── requirements.txt            # Root-level dependencies (Streamlit, legacy)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Variable Required Description Default SECRET_KEY No Flask secret key for sessions iwmi-leaf-secret-key OPENAI_API_KEY No OpenAI API key (required for AI recommendations) — PORT No Server port (used by Render) 5000

Note

The app works fully without OPENAI_API_KEY — only the AI recommendation feature requires it. All other endpoints (map, feasibility, export) work out of the box.

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:5000/health
# → {"status": "healthy"}

docs.title: List available interventions
docs.text: curl http://localhost:5000/api/interventions

docs.title: Get all blocks as GeoJSON
docs.text: curl http://localhost:5000/api/blocks | python -m json.tool | head -20

docs.title: Calculate feasibility for an intervention
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:5000/api/calculate-feasibility \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"intervention": "Organic Farming", "logic": "AND"}'

docs.title: Get protected areas overlay
docs.text: curl http://localhost:5000/api/protected-areas/geojson | python -m json.tool | head -30

docs.title: 1. Select an Intervention
docs.text: Choose an intervention from the dropdown (e.g., "Organic Farming"). The map immediately colors all blocks by feasibility score.

docs.title: 2. Filter by District
docs.text: Optionally select a district to zoom in and filter the distribution chart to that district's blocks only.

docs.title: 3. Configure Variables
docs.text: Click Configure to open the modal. Adjust variable ranges (drag sliders), weights, and add/remove variables. Click Apply to recalculate.

docs.title: 4. Toggle Map Overlays
docs.text: Check the Protected Areas checkbox above the map to overlay national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, and conservation reserves.

docs.title: 5. Explore Block Details
docs.text: Click any block on the map to open its detail view. See all variable values grouped by category (Land & Agriculture, Water, Infrastructure, Livestock, People & Collectives), with out-of-range indicators.

docs.title: 6. Drill Down to GPs
docs.text: When viewing a block in Tinsukia district, a GP dropdown appears. Select a GP to see village-level indicators.

docs.title: 7. Get AI Recommendations
docs.text: In the block detail view, click the robot icon to generate an AI-powered recommendation backed by policy document citations.

docs.title: 8. Export Data
docs.text: Click the CSV button in the filter bar to download the current dataset (with feasibility scores if an intervention is selected).

docs.title: Using the Swagger UI
docs.text:
1. Navigate to http://localhost:5000/docs
  2. Endpoints are grouped by tag: Blocks, Locations, GPs, Interventions, Variables, Feasibility, AI, Export, Config
  3. Click any endpoint to expand it and see parameters + response schema
  4. Click Try it out to enable editing
  5. Fill in parameters and click Execute to make a live request
  6. View the response body, headers, and cURL command

POST Endpoints

For POST endpoints (Feasibility, AI, Export), edit the JSON body in the text area before clicking Execute. The Swagger UI pre-fills a template based on the schema.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Solution ModuleNotFoundError Run pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt Shapefile not found Ensure data files exist in leaf_flask/data/ AI recommendations return 503 Set OPENAI_API_KEY and run /api/ai-recommendation/init Documentation 404 Run mkdocs build from the project root to generate site/ Map shows no blocks Check that 4DSS_VAR_2.0.shp and associated .dbf/.shx files exist GP dropdown missing GP data is only available for Tinsukia — select that district first

docs.title: AI Recommendations API
docs.text:
AI-powered, context-aware recommendations using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The system combines block-level data with policy documents to generate actionable, citation-backed recommendations for specific interventions.

Prerequisites

The AI recommendation feature requires:

  • OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable set on the server
  • RAG dependencies installed (openai, chromadb, langchain, langchain-openai)
  • Vector store initialized (call /api/ai-recommendation/init at least once)
  • PDF policy documents placed in the ai-docs/ directory

All other LEAF DSS features work without these prerequisites.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
User Request
  ↓
Block Data (name, district, intervention, metrics, score)
  ↓
ChromaDB Vector Store ← PDF Policy Documents (ai-docs/)
  ↓
Relevant Policy Context Retrieved (similarity search)
  ↓
OpenAI GPT generates recommendation with context
  ↓
Response: recommendation text + source document citations

The RAG pipeline:

  1. Ingestion — PDFs in ai-docs/ are split into chunks and embedded into ChromaDB using OpenAI embeddings
  2. Retrieval — When a recommendation is requested, the system queries ChromaDB for chunks most relevant to the block's context
  3. Generation — Retrieved context + block data are sent to OpenAI GPT, which generates a tailored recommendation
  4. Citation — Source documents and page numbers are returned alongside the recommendation

docs.title: Get AI Recommendation
docs.text:
Generates a context-aware recommendation for a specific block and intervention. The recommendation considers the block's actual variable values, which criteria are met vs. not met, and relevant policy document context.

POST /api/ai-recommendation

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "block_name": "Digboi",
  "district_name": "Tinsukia",
  "intervention": "Organic Farming",
  "feasibility_score": 65.5,
  "metrics": [
    {
      "label": "Agricultural Land %",
      "value": 45.2,
      "in_range": true,
      "min": 30,
      "max": 70
    },
    {
      "label": "Water Availability",
      "value": 15.0,
      "in_range": false,
      "min": 20,
      "max": 50
    }
  ],
  "filters": [
    {
      "column": "AD",
      "min_val": 30,
      "max_val": 70,
      "weight": 1.0
    }
  ]
}
Field Type Required Description block_name string No Block name (default "Unknown") district_name string No District name (default "Unknown") intervention string Yes Selected intervention name feasibility_score number No Current feasibility score (0–100) metrics array No Variable metrics with in-range status — helps the AI understand which criteria pass/fail metrics[].label string — Human-readable variable name metrics[].value number — Current value for this block metrics[].in_range boolean — Whether value falls in acceptable range metrics[].min number — Minimum threshold metrics[].max number — Maximum threshold filters array No Active filter configurations

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "recommendation": "Based on the analysis of Digboi block in Tinsukia district, Organic Farming shows moderate feasibility (65.5%). The block has good agricultural diversity (45.2%) within the acceptable range. However, water availability (15.0%) falls below the minimum threshold of 20%. Recommendations: 1) Invest in water harvesting structures to improve availability. 2) Focus on drought-resistant organic crop varieties...",
  "sources": [
    {
      "document": "Assam_Agriculture_Policy_2023.pdf",
      "page": 12,
      "relevance": 0.89
    },
    {
      "document": "NABARD_Organic_Farming_Guidelines.pdf",
      "page": 5,
      "relevance": 0.82
    }
  ]
}
Field Type Description recommendation string Generated recommendation text (Markdown-formatted) sources array Policy documents cited in the recommendation sources[].document string PDF filename sources[].page integer Page number in the PDF sources[].relevance number Similarity score (0–1)

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Description 503 RAG service unavailable — dependencies not installed or API key missing 500 Processing error during recommendation generation

docs.title: Example
Initialize Vector Store
docs.text:
Rebuilds the RAG vector store by processing all PDF policy documents from the ai-docs/ directory. This must be called at least once before the first AI recommendation request.

The process:

  1. Scans ai-docs/ for PDF files
  2. Extracts text from each PDF using pypdf
  3. Splits text into overlapping chunks
  4. Generates embeddings using OpenAI's embedding model
  5. Stores embeddings in ChromaDB (local persistent storage)
POST /api/ai-recommendation/init

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: No body required.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "success": true
}

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Response Description 503 {"success": false, "error": "..."} RAG dependencies not installed 500 {"success": false, "error": "..."} Error processing PDF documents

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl -X POST https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/ai-recommendation/init

import requests

r = requests.post("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/ai-recommendation/init")
result = r.json()
if result['success']:
    print("Vector store initialized successfully")
else:
    print(f"Error: {result.get('error')}")

When to Re-initialize

Re-run initialization whenever you:

  • Add new PDF documents to ai-docs/
  • Update or replace existing PDFs
  • Remove documents from the directory
  • Upgrade the embedding model

The process takes 30–120 seconds depending on the number and size of PDFs.

docs.title: Blocks API
docs.text: Block-level geospatial data and lookups. Blocks are administrative subdivisions within districts — there are approximately 152 blocks across all 27 districts in Assam. Each block carries dozens of indicator variables covering agriculture, water, infrastructure, livestock, and socio-economic factors.

docs.title: Get All Blocks
docs.text:
Returns all blocks as a GeoJSON FeatureCollection with all variable data as feature properties. This is the primary data endpoint used by the map frontend.

GET /api/blocks
GET /api/blocks/geojson

Both paths return identical data.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "type": "FeatureCollection",
  "features": [
    {
      "type": "Feature",
      "properties": {
        "BLOCK_ID": "1234",
        "Block_name": "Digboi",
        "Dist_Name": "Tinsukia",
        "DISTRICT_I": "134",
        "AD": 45.2,
        "WA": 30.1,
        "CF": 62.3,
        "CI": 28.7
      },
      "geometry": {
        "type": "Polygon",
        "coordinates": [[[95.1, 27.3], [95.2, 27.3], [95.2, 27.4], [95.1, 27.3]]]
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Key Property Fields
docs.text:
Field Type Description BLOCK_ID string Unique block identifier Block_name string Human-readable block name Dist_Name string District name (mapped from DISTRICT_I) DISTRICT_I string District ID code Variable codes number Indicator values (AD, WA, CF, CI, etc.) — see Variables API for metadata

Geometry

Geometries are simplified (tolerance 0.001) for web performance. Coordinates are in WGS84 (EPSG:4326). For full-resolution geometries, access the raw shapefile directly.

docs.title: Example
Get Block by ID
docs.text:
Returns a single block's GeoJSON by its BLOCK_ID field.

GET /api/blocks/{block_id}

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter In Type Required Description block_id path string Yes The BLOCK_ID identifier

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Returns a GeoJSON FeatureCollection containing the single matching block with all properties and geometry.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Description 404 No block found with the given BLOCK_ID

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/blocks/1234

import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/blocks/1234")
if r.status_code == 200:
    block = r.json()
    props = block['features'][0]['properties']
    print(f"Block: {props['Block_name']}, District: {props['Dist_Name']}")
else:
    print(f"Error: {r.json()['error']}")

docs.title: Get Block by Name
docs.text:
Returns a single block's GeoJSON by its Block_name value.

GET /api/blocks/by-name/{block_name}

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter In Type Required Description block_name path string Yes Block name, case-sensitive (e.g. "Digboi")

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Returns a GeoJSON FeatureCollection for the matching block.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Description 404 No block found with the given name

docs.title: Example
Get Block Statistics
docs.text:
Returns aggregate statistics for the block dataset including district distribution and available data columns.

GET /api/blocks/statistics
GET /api/statistics

Both paths return identical data.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "total_blocks": 152,
  "districts": {
    "Tinsukia": 8,
    "Dibrugarh": 7,
    "Jorhat": 6,
    "Kamrup Metropolitan": 5
  },
  "district_count": 27,
  "columns": ["BLOCK_ID", "Block_name", "Dist_Name", "DISTRICT_I", "AD", "WA", "CF", "CI"]
}
Field Type Description total_blocks integer Total number of blocks in the dataset districts object Block count per district (key: district name, value: count) district_count integer Number of distinct districts columns array All available data columns (excluding geometry)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/blocks/statistics

import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/blocks/statistics")
stats = r.json()
print(f"Total blocks: {stats['total_blocks']}")
print(f"Districts: {stats['district_count']}")
print(f"Variables available: {len(stats['columns'])}")

# Top 5 districts by block count
sorted_districts = sorted(stats['districts'].items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
for name, count in sorted_districts[:5]:
    print(f"  {name}: {count} blocks")

Use for Discovery

The columns field is useful for discovering which variables are available before calling the Variables API for full metadata.

docs.title: Example
Get Available Data Levels
docs.text:
Returns the data hierarchy levels (block, GP) and which districts support each level. Use this to determine whether to show GP-level options in your UI.

GET /api/levels

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "levels": [
    {
      "id": "block",
      "name": "Block",
      "available": true
    },
    {
      "id": "gp",
      "name": "Gram Panchayat",
      "available": true,
      "districts": ["Tinsukia"]
    }
  ],
  "gp_districts": ["Tinsukia"]

Field Type Description levels array Available data levels levels[].id string Level identifier (block or gp) levels[].name string Display name levels[].available boolean Whether this level has data loaded levels[].districts array Districts supporting this level (GP only) gp_districts array Shortcut — list of districts with GP-level data

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/levels

import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/levels")
data = r.json()

for level in data['levels']:
    districts = level.get('districts', ['All'])
    print(f"{level['name']}: available={level['available']}, districts={districts}")

docs.title: Get Protected Areas GeoJSON
docs.text:
Returns Protected Areas of India (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, conservation reserves, community reserves, and tiger reserves) as a GeoJSON FeatureCollection. This dataset covers all of India (~550 protected areas) and is used as a map overlay layer to show conservation zones alongside agricultural feasibility data.

GET /api/protected-areas/geojson

docs.title: Protected Area Types
docs.text: Type Count (approx.) Description National Park ~100 Highest protection level, no human habitation Wildlife Sanctuary ~400 Controlled human activities permitted Conservation Reserve ~30 Community-managed conservation areas Tiger Reserve ~15 Special tiger conservation zones Community Reserve ~5 Community-owned conservation areas

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Overlay on feasibility map — Identify blocks/GPs that overlap with protected areas, which may affect intervention planning
  • Environmental screening — Filter out areas where certain agricultural activities are restricted
  • Conservation context — Understand the ecological context around proposed intervention areas

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Description 404 Protected areas shapefile not available 500 Server error during shapefile processing

docs.text:
Returns service health status. Use this for uptime monitoring and load balancer health checks.

GET /health

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "status": "healthy"
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/health

import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/health")
print(f"Status: {r.json()['status']}")

Monitoring

Point your uptime monitor (UptimeRobot, Render health checks, Pingdom, etc.) at /health for reliable service monitoring. The endpoint has no dependencies — it returns immediately without loading data.

docs.title: Export API
docs.text: Export filtered block data as downloadable CSV files. Supports exporting raw data or data with calculated feasibility scores.

docs.title: Export Data as CSV
docs.text:
Exports block data as a CSV file. If an intervention or custom filters are provided, feasibility scores are calculated and included as additional columns.

POST /api/export/csv

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "intervention": "Organic Farming",
  "filters": [
    {
      "column": "AD",
      "min_val": 20,
      "max_val": 60,
      "weight": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "logic": "AND"
}
Field Type Required Description intervention string No Intervention name — uses its default config if no custom filters provided filters array No Custom filter criteria logic string No "AND" or "OR", default "AND"

Export Modes

  • With intervention/filters: CSV includes feasibility_score and feasibility_class columns calculated from the criteria
  • Without intervention/filters: CSV contains raw block data only (all variables, no scores)

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
• Content-Type: text/csv
  • Content-Disposition: attachment; filename=leaf_data.csv
  • Body: CSV text with headers

The CSV includes all block data columns (BLOCK_ID, Block_name, Dist_Name, all variable codes). If feasibility was calculated, two additional columns appear:

Column Type Description feasibility_score number Score 0–100 (only when filters applied) feasibility_class string Class label: Very High, High, Moderate High, Moderate, Low, Very Low

docs.title: Example
Feasibility API
docs.text: Calculate weighted feasibility scores for agricultural interventions at both block and GP levels. This is the core analytical engine of LEAF DSS — it evaluates how suitable each spatial unit is for a given intervention based on multiple variable criteria.

docs.title: How Feasibility Scoring Works
docs.text:
The scoring algorithm follows these steps:

  1. Define criteria — Provide variable filters with acceptable min/max ranges and importance weights
  2. Evaluate each feature — For each block/GP, check if each variable falls within the specified range
  3. Calculate score — Weighted sum of criteria met, normalized to 0–100
Score = Σ (variable_in_range × weight) / Σ (weight) × 100
  1. Classify — Scores are bucketed into feasibility classes:
Score Range Class Color 80–100 Very High Dark Green 60–80 High Green 45–60 Moderate High Light Green 30–45 Moderate Yellow 15–30 Low Orange 0–15 Very Low Red
  1. Return — GeoJSON with scores embedded as properties, plus distribution statistics

docs.title: Calculate Block Feasibility
docs.text:
Calculates feasibility scores for all blocks (~152) based on provided filters.

POST /api/calculate-feasibility

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "intervention": "Organic Farming",
  "filters": [
    {
      "column": "AD",
      "min_val": 20,
      "max_val": 60,
      "weight": 1.0
    },
    {
      "column": "WA",
      "min_val": 30,
      "max_val": 70,
      "weight": 0.8
    }
  ],
  "logic": "AND",
  "district": "134"
}
Field Type Required Description intervention string No Intervention name — uses its default config if no filters provided filters array No Custom filter criteria (overrides intervention defaults if both provided) filters[].column string Yes Variable column name (e.g. AD, WA) filters[].min_val number Yes Minimum acceptable value filters[].max_val number Yes Maximum acceptable value filters[].weight number No Importance weight (0–1), default 1.0 logic string No "AND" or "OR", default "AND" district string No District ID to filter statistics to a specific district

Intervention vs Filters

  • Intervention only: System uses the intervention's pre-configured variables from the CSV
  • Filters only: System uses your custom filters
  • Both: Custom filters take precedence, intervention is ignored
  • Neither: All blocks scored as "no data"

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "geojson": {
    "type": "FeatureCollection",
    "features": [
      {
        "type": "Feature",
        "properties": {
          "Block_name": "Digboi",
          "Dist_Name": "Tinsukia",
          "AD": 45.2,
          "WA": 35.1,
          "feasibility_score": 75.5,
          "feasibility_class": "High",
          "feasibility_color": "#81c784"
        },
        "geometry": { "type": "Polygon", "coordinates": [[...]] }
      }
    ]
  },
  "statistics": {
    "mean_score": 52.3,
    "very_high_count": 12,
    "high_count": 33,
    "moderate_high_count": 28,
    "moderate_count": 35,
    "low_count": 25,
    "very_low_count": 19
  },

    { "column": "AD", "min_val": 20, "max_val": 60, "weight": 1.0 },
    { "column": "WA", "min_val": 30, "max_val": 70, "weight": 0.8 }
  ]
}
Field Type Description geojson object GeoJSON FeatureCollection with all blocks and feasibility properties geojson.features[].properties.feasibility_score number Score 0–100 geojson.features[].properties.feasibility_class string Class label geojson.features[].properties.feasibility_color string Hex color for map display statistics object Distribution counts and mean score filters_applied array Echo of the filters used in calculation

docs.title: Example
Calculate GP Feasibility
docs.text:
Calculates feasibility scores at the Gram Panchayat level. Uses the same scoring algorithm as block feasibility but operates on GP-level data.

POST /api/gp/calculate-feasibility

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "filters": [
    {
      "column": "AD",
      "min_val": 20,
      "max_val": 60,
      "weight": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "logic": "AND",
  "block": "Digboi"
}
Field Type Required Description filters array Yes Filter criteria (same format as block feasibility) logic string No "AND" or "OR", default "AND" block string No Block name to filter — only calculates for GPs in this block

Block Filter

The block parameter is useful for focused analysis. Without it, all ~95 GPs in Tinsukia are scored. With it, only GPs in the specified block are included.

docs.title: Response
docs.text: Same format as block feasibility — GeoJSON with scores and distribution statistics.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Code Description 404 GP data not available

docs.title: Example
Gram Panchayats API
docs.text:
GP-level geospatial data and lookups. Gram Panchayats (GPs) are village-level administrative units within blocks — the finest spatial resolution in the LEAF DSS data hierarchy.

Data Availability

GP-level data is currently available for Tinsukia district only (~95 GPs across 8 blocks). For all other districts, use the Blocks API for block-level data. Use GET /api/levels to check which districts support GP data.

GP data includes variables from the DSS metadata (same codes as block-level: AD, WA, etc.) plus GP-specific indicators for livestock density, transport connectivity, finance, utilities, and agricultural activities.

docs.text:

GET /api/gp
GET /api/gp/geojson

Both paths return identical data.

{
  "type": "FeatureCollection",

        "Block_Name": "Digboi",
        "Dist_Name": "Tinsukia",

        "AD": 42.1,
        "BW": 8
      },
      "geometry": {
        "type": "Polygon",
        "coordinates": [[[95.4, 27.4], ...]]

}

docs.title: Interventions API
docs.text:
Agricultural intervention definitions and their variable configurations. Interventions represent specific agricultural strategies (Organic Farming, Micro-Irrigation, etc.) that can be evaluated for feasibility across blocks and GPs.

Interventions are auto-detected from the Cluster column in the DSS metadata CSV (DSS_input2.csv). Each intervention has a set of associated variables with predefined acceptable ranges, weights, and preference directions.

docs.title: How Interventions Work
docs.text:
1. Definition — Each intervention groups related variables that determine its feasibility
  2. Configuration — Each variable has a range (min/max), weight (importance), and preference (higher/lower/moderate)
  3. Scoring — When a user selects an intervention, the system applies its variable config as filters to the feasibility calculation
  4. Customization — Users can override the default config in the UI's Configure modal
Intervention "Organic Farming"
  └─ Variable AD (Agricultural Diversity): range 20–80, weight 1.0, prefer higher
  └─ Variable WA (Water Availability): range 30–70, weight 0.8, prefer moderate
  └─ Variable CF (Crop Finance): range 10–60, weight 0.6, prefer higher

}

docs.title: API Overview
docs.text: The LEAF DSS API provides RESTful endpoints for geospatial data, feasibility analysis, and AI recommendations. All API routes are prefixed with /api/ except the health check at /health.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text:
Production: https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com
Local:      http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: The API is open and unauthenticated — no API keys or tokens are required. All endpoints are publicly accessible. The only exception is the AI recommendation endpoint, which requires an OPENAI_API_KEY to be set on the server side (not passed by the client).

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text:
All endpoints return JSON (Content-Type: application/json) except the CSV export endpoint which returns text/csv.

Successful responses return data directly. Errors return:

{
  "error": "Description of what went wrong"
}

docs.title: CORS
docs.text: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing is enabled for all origins via flask-cors. You can call the API from any frontend application, Jupyter notebook, or script without CORS issues.

docs.title: Endpoint Categories
docs.text:
Category Prefix Endpoints Description Blocks /api/blocks 4 Block-level geospatial data and lookups Locations /api/locations, /api/districts 4 Hierarchical location data (districts, blocks, GPs) Gram Panchayats /api/gp 8 GP-level geospatial data and lookups Interventions /api/interventions 2 Intervention definitions and variable configs Variables /api/variables 3 Variable metadata and statistics Feasibility /api/calculate-feasibility 2 Weighted feasibility score calculation AI /api/ai-recommendation 2 RAG-powered AI recommendations Export /api/export 1 CSV data export Config /api/config, /health 5 App config, health, map overlays

Total: 31 endpoints across 9 categories.

docs.title: GeoJSON Responses
docs.text:
Geospatial endpoints return standard GeoJSON FeatureCollections. Each feature includes a properties object with all variable data and a geometry object with polygon coordinates in WGS84 (EPSG:4326).

{
  "type": "FeatureCollection",
  "features": [
    {
      "type": "Feature",
      "properties": {
        "Block_name": "Digboi",
        "Dist_Name": "Tinsukia",
        "BLOCK_ID": "1234",
        "AD": 45.2,
        "WA": 30.1
      },
      "geometry": {
        "type": "Polygon",
        "coordinates": [[[95.1, 27.3], [95.2, 27.3], [95.2, 27.4], [95.1, 27.3]]]
      }
    }
  ]
}

Working with GeoJSON

GeoJSON responses can be directly loaded into mapping libraries like Leaflet, Mapbox, or QGIS. In Python, use geopandas.GeoDataFrame.from_features(response['features']) to convert to a GeoDataFrame.

docs.title: Filter Criteria
docs.text:
POST endpoints that calculate feasibility accept an array of filter criteria:

{
  "column": "AD",
  "min_val": 20,
  "max_val": 60,
  "weight": 1.0
}
Field Type Required Description column string Yes Variable/column name from the shapefile min_val number Yes Minimum acceptable threshold max_val number Yes Maximum acceptable threshold weight number No Importance weight (0–1), default 1.0

docs.title: Logic Modes
docs.text:
When multiple filters are provided, you can choose how they combine:

Mode Behavior Use Case AND (default) Block must meet all criteria to score highly Strict screening — only blocks meeting every requirement OR Block scores based on any criteria met Lenient screening — blocks meeting at least one requirement

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: HTTP Code Meaning When 200 Success Normal response 404 Resource not found Invalid block ID, GP name, or missing data files 500 Server error Unexpected processing error 503 Service unavailable AI/RAG dependencies not configured

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text: There are no rate limits on the API. However, some endpoints (particularly GET /api/blocks and POST /api/calculate-feasibility) return large GeoJSON payloads (1–5 MB). Consider caching responses client-side when building frontend applications.

docs.title: Interactive Docs
docs.text:
Two documentation interfaces are available:

Interface URL Description Swagger UI /docs Interactive API explorer — try endpoints live with "Try it out" MkDocs /documentation/ This written documentation with guides and examples

docs.title: Get all blocks and count them (Python)
docs.text:
import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/blocks")
geojson = r.json()
print(f"Total blocks: {len(geojson['features'])}")

# Get unique districts
districts = set(f['properties']['Dist_Name'] for f in geojson['features'])
print(f"Districts: {len(districts)}")

docs.title: Run feasibility analysis (JavaScript)
docs.text:
const response = await fetch('/api/calculate-feasibility', {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    intervention: 'Organic Farming',
    logic: 'AND'
  })
});

console.log(`Mean score: ${result.statistics.mean_score}`);
console.log(`High feasibility blocks: ${result.statistics.high_count}`);

docs.title: Variables API
docs.text:
Metadata and statistics for the data variables (indicators) available in the shapefiles. Variables represent measurable characteristics of each block or GP — agriculture percentages, water indices, infrastructure counts, livestock densities, and more.

Each variable has a short code (e.g., AD, WA, CF) used as column names in the shapefile and GeoJSON properties. These endpoints provide human-readable labels, descriptions, group categories, and statistical ranges.

docs.title: Get All Block-Level Variables
docs.text:
Returns metadata for every numeric variable in the block-level shapefile, including min/max/mean statistics computed across all ~152 blocks.

GET /api/variables

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
[
  {
    "field": "AD",
    "label": "Agricultural Diversity",
    "description": "Percentage of agricultural land diversity",
    "group": "Land & Agriculture",
    "data_min": 0.0,
    "data_max": 95.5,
    "data_mean": 42.3
  },
  {
    "field": "WA",
    "label": "Water Availability",
    "description": "Water availability index",
    "group": "Water Resources",
    "data_min": 5.0,
    "data_max": 88.0,
    "data_mean": 35.7
  }
]
Field Type Description field string Column code in the shapefile (used in filter criteria) label string Human-readable name description string What this variable measures group string Category group for UI organization data_min number Minimum value across all blocks data_max number Maximum value across all blocks data_mean number Mean value across all blocks

docs.title: Variable Groups
docs.text:
Variables are organized into thematic groups:

Group Description Example Variables Land & Agriculture Crop patterns, soil quality, agricultural land use AD, CF, CI Water Resources Water availability, irrigation, groundwater WA, GW Infrastructure Roads, markets, connectivity, facilities IN, RD Livestock Animal populations, livestock density LV, CT People & Collectives Demographics, SHGs, cooperatives PO, SH

docs.title: Example
Get Variable Groups
docs.text:
Returns the list of variable group categories used to organize variables in the UI.

GET /api/variable-groups

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "groups": [
    "Land & Agriculture",
    "Water Resources",
    "Infrastructure",
    "Livestock",
    "People & Collectives"
  ]
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
cURLPython 
curl https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/variable-groups

import requests

r = requests.get("https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api/variable-groups")
groups = r.json()['groups']
print(f"Available groups: {', '.join(groups)}")

docs.title: Get Variable Statistics
docs.text:
Returns min, max, and mean values for a specific variable column. Supports both block-level and GP-level data via the optional level query parameter.

GET /api/variable-stats/{variable}

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter In Type Required Description variable path string Yes Variable column code (e.g. AD, WA, CF)

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "min": 0.0,
  "max": 95.5,
  "mean": 42.3
}
Field Type Description min number Minimum value across all features max number Maximum value across all features mean number Mean value across all features

Missing Variables

If the variable name doesn't exist in the shapefile, the endpoint returns default values {min: 0, max: 100, mean: 50} instead of an error.

docs.title: Example
Architecture
docs.text: LEAF DSS is a monolithic Flask application serving both the web UI and REST API from a single process. This design simplifies deployment while keeping the codebase maintainable through clear module separation.

docs.title: Key Modules
docs.text: Module File Responsibility App app.py Flask routes (31+ endpoints), request handling, Flasgger Swagger config, MkDocs serving Config config.py Color palettes, variable group definitions, map center/zoom settings Data Utils data_utils.py Shapefile loading (block, GP, district, protected areas), GeoJSON conversion, variable metadata extraction, GP-block mapping Feasibility feasibility.py Weighted score calculation, classification into feasibility classes, distribution statistics RAG Utils rag_utils.py PDF processing, ChromaDB vector store management, OpenAI recommendation generation

docs.title: Initial Page Load
docs.text:
Browser → GET /
  └→ render_template(index.html) with interventions, config
Browser → GET /api/blocks (or /api/gp for GP level)
  └→ load_shapefile() → GeoDataFrame → GeoJSON
Browser → GET /api/districts/geojson
  └→ load_district_boundaries() → GeoJSON
Browser → GET /api/protected-areas/geojson (if toggled on)
  └→ load_protected_areas() → GeoJSON
Browser → Leaflet.js renders all layers

docs.title: Feasibility Calculation
docs.text:
Browser → POST /api/calculate-feasibility
  └→ Parse { intervention, filters, logic, district } from JSON body
  └→ load_shapefile() → GeoDataFrame (cached)
  └→ If intervention only: load filters from get_intervention_config()
  └→ add_feasibility_to_gdf(gdf, filters, logic)
      └→ For each block: score = Σ(in_range × weight) / Σ(weight)
      └→ Classify scores into feasibility classes
  └→ calculate_and_get_geojson() → scored GeoJSON + distribution stats
  └→ Return JSON response
Browser → Update map colors, chart, and filter indicators

docs.title: AI Recommendation
docs.text:
Browser → POST /api/ai-recommendation
  └→ Lazy-import rag_utils (first call only)
  └→ Build context string from block data + metrics
  └→ Query ChromaDB for relevant policy document chunks
  └→ Construct prompt: context + retrieved chunks + instruction
  └→ Send to OpenAI GPT
  └→ Return { recommendation, sources }

docs.title: URL-Based Navigation
docs.text:
Browser → GET /Tinsukia/Digboi
  └→ render_app(level='block', district='Tinsukia', block='Digboi')
  └→ Frontend reads window.INITIAL_STATE
  └→ Applies district filter → zooms map → opens block detail

docs.title: Caching Strategy
docs.text:
Data loading functions use @lru_cache(maxsize=1) to cache results in memory after first access:

Function What's Cached Approx. Size load_shapefile() Block GeoDataFrame (152 rows) ~2 MB load_gp_shapefile() GP GeoDataFrame (95 rows) ~1 MB load_district_boundaries() District GeoJSON dict ~500 KB load_protected_areas() Protected areas GeoJSON dict ~5 MB load_metadata() Variable CSV DataFrame ~100 KB load_district_mapping() District ID→name dict ~1 KB load_gp_data() GP indicator DataFrame ~50 KB load_gp_block_mapping() GP name→block dict ~5 KB

Cache is populated on first request and persists for the lifetime of the process. On Render, this means until the next deployment or dyno restart.

Cache Invalidation

There is no cache invalidation mechanism. If data files change, the server must be restarted. In development, the Flask debug reloader handles this automatically.

docs.title: Frontend Architecture
docs.text:
The frontend is a single-page application (SPA) built with vanilla JavaScript:

Component Technology File Map Leaflet.js 1.9.4 static/js/app.js Charts Chart.js 4.4.1 static/js/app.js Icons Bootstrap Icons 1.11.3 CDN Fonts Inter (Google Fonts) CDN Layout CSS Grid + Flexbox static/css/style.css

docs.title: Global State Object
docs.text:
All application state is stored in a single state object (app.js:10):

  • state.map — Leaflet map instance
  • state.geojsonLayer — Current data layer (blocks or GPs)
  • state.districtLayer — District boundary overlay
  • state.protectedAreasLayer — Protected areas overlay
  • state.currentIntervention — Selected intervention name
  • state.currentFilters — Active filter array
  • state.currentLevel — 'block' or 'gp'
  • state.activeVariable — Variable for single-variable choropleth mode

docs.title: Map Layers (z-order, bottom to top)
docs.text:
1. CartoDB Light tile layer (base map)
  2. state.geojsonLayer — Feasibility-colored blocks/GPs
  3. state.protectedAreasLayer — Green protected area polygons (optional)
  4. state.districtLayer — Dashed district boundaries

docs.title: Shapefiles (in leaf_flask/data/)
docs.text: File Description Records Coverage 4DSS_VAR_2.0.shp Block-level indicators ~152 All Assam Block_assam.shp District-block mapping ~152 All Assam grampanchayat.shp GP boundaries ~95 Tinsukia districts.geojson District boundaries 27 All Assam protected_areas/Protected_Area_India_Final.shp Protected areas ~550 All India

docs.title: CSV / Excel Data
docs.text: File Description DSS_input2.csv Variable metadata, intervention configs (Cluster), group mappings, labels, descriptions Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx GP-level indicator values (coded column headers matching DSS variables) Block_GPs.xlsx GP-to-block mapping table Vill_Gp_join.csv Village-to-GP mapping with GP_CODE

docs.title: AI Documents (in ai-docs/)
docs.text:
• PDF policy documents for RAG pipeline
  • Processed into ChromaDB vector store on initialization
  • Used for context-aware AI recommendations

docs.title: API Route Organization
docs.text:
Routes in app.py are organized by section (in order):

  1. Page Routes — HTML template rendering (/, /<district>, /<district>/<block>, /<district>/<block>/<gp>)
  2. Block APIs — GET /api/blocks/*
  3. Location APIs — GET /api/locations, /api/districts/*
  4. Protected Areas — GET /api/protected-areas/geojson
  5. Intervention APIs — GET /api/interventions, /api/intervention/<name>/config
  6. Variable APIs — GET /api/variables, /api/variable-groups, /api/variable-stats/*
  7. Feasibility APIs — POST /api/calculate-feasibility, POST /api/gp/calculate-feasibility
  8. AI APIs — POST /api/ai-recommendation, POST /api/ai-recommendation/init
  9. Export API — POST /api/export/csv
  10. Config APIs — GET /api/config, /api/levels
  11. GP APIs — GET /api/gp/*, POST /api/gp/calculate-feasibility
  12. Health & Meta — GET /health, GET /api
  13. Static Serving — /ai-docs/*, /documentation/*
  14. Error Handlers — 404 (returns main app for non-API routes), 500

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: LEAF DSS can be deployed to cloud platforms (Render recommended) or run locally for development.

docs.title: Render (Production)
docs.text: The app is deployed on Render as a Web Service.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Setting Value Runtime Python Build Command pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt && pip install mkdocs-material && mkdocs build Start Command cd leaf_flask && gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT Plan Free or Starter

Build Command

The build command installs MkDocs and builds the documentation site into site/, which Flask then serves at /documentation/. If you don't need the docs site, simplify to just pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Set these in the Render dashboard under Environment:

Variable Required Description SECRET_KEY Yes Flask secret key (generate a random string) OPENAI_API_KEY No Required only for AI recommendations PYTHON_VERSION No Python version (e.g. 3.11.0)

docs.title: Deploy Steps
docs.text:
1. Push code to GitHub
  2. Create a new Web Service in Render dashboard
  3. Connect your GitHub repository
  4. Set the Build and Start commands as above
  5. Add environment variables
  6. Click Deploy

Render auto-deploys on every push to the main branch.

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
Configure Render's health check to use: 

GET /health

docs.title: Available URLs (Production)
docs.text: URL Description https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/ Main dashboard (map UI) https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/docs Swagger API documentation https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ MkDocs written documentation https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/api API endpoint listing https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/health Health check

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
cd leaf_flask
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app.py

Runs on http://localhost:5000 with debug mode and auto-reload enabled.

docs.title: With Documentation Site
docs.text:
# From project root
pip install mkdocs-material
mkdocs build                    # Generates site/ directory
cd leaf_flask
python app.py                   # Serves docs at /documentation/

docs.title: MkDocs Live Preview
docs.text:
For editing documentation with live preview:

mkdocs serve                    # Serves at http://localhost:8000

This runs a separate MkDocs dev server with hot-reload — changes to docs/*.md reflect instantly.

docs.title: Documentation Site
docs.text: The MkDocs documentation is served directly from the Flask app at /documentation/. This means both the API and its documentation are available from the same URL.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. mkdocs build generates a static HTML site in site/
  2. Flask serves these files via the /documentation/ route
  3. MkDocs is configured with site_url: https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ so all internal links resolve correctly

docs.title: Rebuilding Documentation
docs.text:
After editing any file in docs/:

mkdocs build          # Regenerate site/ directory

The Flask app serves from site/ — no restart needed (files are served dynamically).

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
MkDocs configuration is in mkdocs.yml at the project root:

Setting Value Purpose site_url https://iwmi-leaf.onrender.com/documentation/ Ensures internal links work under /documentation/ theme.name material Material for MkDocs theme plugins search Full-text search in documentation

docs.title: File Requirements
docs.text: Ensure these data files are present in leaf_flask/data/:

docs.title: Required (core functionality)
docs.text: File(s) Description 4DSS_VAR_2.0.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj Block-level shapefile with indicator data Block_assam.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj District-block mapping shapefile DSS_input2.csv Variable metadata and intervention config

docs.title: Optional (enhanced features)
docs.text: File(s) Description Feature grampanchayat.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj GP boundaries (Tinsukia) GP-level views Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx GP indicator data GP variables Block_GPs.xlsx GP-to-block mapping GP block grouping Vill_Gp_join.csv Village-GP mapping GP code resolution districts.geojson District boundaries Map overlay protected_areas/Protected_Area_India_Final.shp + .dbf, .shx, .prj, .cst Protected Areas of India Map overlay

docs.title: AI features
docs.text: File(s) Description ai-docs/*.pdf Policy documents for RAG pipeline

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Issue Solution App won't start Check pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt completed without errors Shapefile not found Verify .shp, .dbf, .shx, and .prj files all exist in leaf_flask/data/ AI returns 503 Set OPENAI_API_KEY env var and call POST /api/ai-recommendation/init Docs return 404 Run mkdocs build from the project root to generate the site/ directory Slow first request Normal — first request loads and caches all shapefiles (~3-5 seconds) GP data missing GP data only exists for Tinsukia — ensure GP shapefiles and Excel files are present Render deploy fails Check build logs — most common issue is missing Python version or system dependencies

docs.title: Hosting & Handover Guide
docs.text: This guide is for anyone who needs to host LEAF DSS independently — whether you're a government IT team, an NGO partner, a research institution, or a developer taking over the project. It covers what you need, your hosting options, estimated costs, and step-by-step instructions.

docs.title: What You're Hosting
docs.text: LEAF DSS is a single Python web application that serves both the interactive map UI and the REST API. There is no separate frontend deployment — everything runs from one process.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
Resource Minimum Recommended CPU 1 vCPU 2 vCPUs RAM 512 MB 1–2 GB Disk 500 MB 1 GB Python 3.10+ 3.11 or 3.12 OS Any Linux Ubuntu 22.04+

RAM is the main constraint — the app loads shapefiles into memory (~10–15 MB cached). With the AI/RAG feature enabled, RAM usage increases to ~500 MB due to ChromaDB and embeddings.

docs.title: What's Included
docs.text: Component Description Required? Flask web app Map UI + REST API + Swagger docs Yes Shapefiles & CSV Block/GP/district/protected area geodata Yes MkDocs site Written documentation (pre-built HTML) Optional AI/RAG pipeline Policy document recommendations Optional PDF policy docs Source documents for AI Only if AI enabled

docs.title: Option 1: Render (Current Setup) — Recommended for Getting Started
docs.text:
Render is a managed platform that auto-deploys from GitHub.

Plan Monthly Cost RAM CPU Notes Free $0 512 MB Shared Sleeps after 15 min inactivity, cold starts ~30s Starter $7/month 512 MB Shared Always on, no sleep Standard $25/month 2 GB 1 vCPU Recommended for production Pro $85/month 4 GB 2 vCPUs For heavy AI/RAG usage

Pros: Zero server management, auto-deploy from Git, free SSL, health checks. Cons: Free tier has cold starts; US/EU regions only.

docs.title: Setup Steps
docs.text:
1. Push code to a GitHub repository
  2. Sign up at render.com
  3. Click New → Web Service → connect your GitHub repo
  4. Configure:
    • Build Command: pip install -r leaf_flask/requirements.txt && pip install mkdocs-material && mkdocs build
    • Start Command: cd leaf_flask && gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:$PORT --workers 2 --timeout 120
  5. Add environment variables:
    • SECRET_KEY = any random string
    • OPENAI_API_KEY = your OpenAI key (only if using AI features)
  6. Click Deploy

docs.title: Option 2: AWS (EC2 + Elastic Beanstalk or ECS)
docs.text:
Best for organizations already on AWS with IT teams.

Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes EC2 t3.micro ~$8/month 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, free tier eligible (1 year) EC2 t3.small ~$15/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM — recommended Elastic Beanstalk EC2 cost + $0 Managed deployment wrapper around EC2 ECS Fargate ~$15–30/month Serverless containers, no EC2 management Application Load Balancer ~$16/month Add if you need custom domain + SSL Route 53 (DNS) ~$0.50/month Custom domain routing

Total estimate: $15–50/month for a basic production setup.

docs.title: Quick EC2 Setup
docs.text:
# On a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 EC2 instance
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y python3-pip python3-venv nginx

# Clone and setup
git clone <repo-url> /opt/iwmi-leaf
cd /opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install mkdocs-material
cd .. && mkdocs build && cd leaf_flask

# Run with Gunicorn
gunicorn app:app --bind 0.0.0.0:5000 --workers 2 --timeout 120 --daemon

# (Optional) Set up Nginx reverse proxy for port 80/443

Use a process manager

In production, use systemd or supervisord to keep Gunicorn running and auto-restart on crashes. See the systemd section below.

docs.title: Option 3: Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
docs.text:
Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes Cloud Run ~$5–15/month Serverless containers, pay per request, auto-scaling Compute Engine e2-small ~$13/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM App Engine (Standard) ~$0–25/month Managed platform, auto-scales to zero

Cloud Run is the most cost-effective for low-to-moderate traffic.

docs.title: Cloud Run Setup
docs.text:
# Build container
docker build -t iwmi-leaf .
docker tag iwmi-leaf gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf
docker push gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf

# Deploy
gcloud run deploy iwmi-leaf \
  --image gcr.io/YOUR_PROJECT/iwmi-leaf \
  --port 5000 \
  --memory 1Gi \
  --cpu 1 \
  --allow-unauthenticated

docs.title: Option 4: Azure
docs.text: Service Monthly Cost (estimate) Notes App Service B1 ~$13/month 1 vCPU, 1.75 GB RAM App Service B2 ~$26/month 2 vCPU, 3.5 GB RAM Container Instances ~$10–20/month Serverless containers

docs.title: Option 5: DigitalOcean / Hetzner (Budget VPS)
docs.text:
Best value for money if you're comfortable with basic Linux administration.

Provider Plan Monthly Cost Specs DigitalOcean Basic Droplet $6/month 1 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, 25 GB SSD DigitalOcean Regular Droplet $12/month 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM, 50 GB SSD Hetzner CX22 €4/month (~$4.50) 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM, 40 GB — best value DigitalOcean App Platform Basic $5/month Managed platform (like Render)

docs.title: Option 6: On-Premises / Government Data Center
docs.text:
For government agencies or organizations with internal hosting infrastructure.

Requirement Details Server Any Linux server with Python 3.10+, 2 GB RAM Network HTTP/HTTPS access on ports 80/443 SSL Use Let's Encrypt (free) or organization's certificate Backup Only the data/ directory and ai-docs/ need backup Updates Pull from Git and restart the service

docs.title: Cost Summary
docs.text:
Scenario Monthly Cost Best For Free / testing $0 Demos, evaluation (Render free tier) Small production $5–15/month Low traffic, internal use Standard production $15–30/month Public-facing, reliable uptime With AI features $30–50/month Includes OpenAI API usage (~$5–20/month extra) On-premises $0 (infra cost) Government/institutional hosting

OpenAI API Costs

The AI recommendation feature calls the OpenAI API per request. Typical cost is $0.01–0.05 per recommendation. With 100 recommendations/month, expect ~$1–5/month in API fees on top of hosting costs. You can also use a self-hosted LLM to avoid this cost entirely.

docs.title: Handover Checklist
docs.text: If you're handing this project to another team, ensure they have:

docs.title: 1. Source Code
docs.text:
• [ ] Access to the Git repository (GitHub/GitLab)
  • [ ] All branches up to date

docs.title: 2. Data Files
docs.text:
These are NOT in Git (too large). Transfer them separately:

  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/4DSS_VAR_2.0.* (block shapefile — .shp, .dbf, .shx, .prj, .qmd)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Block_assam.* (district mapping shapefile)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/grampanchayat.* (GP shapefile)
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/districts.geojson
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/protected_areas/Protected_Area_India_Final.*
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/DSS_input2.csv
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Block_GPs.xlsx
  • [ ] leaf_flask/data/Vill_Gp_join.csv
  • [ ] ai-docs/*.pdf (policy documents for AI)

docs.title: 3. Environment Variables
docs.text:
• [ ] SECRET_KEY — any random string for Flask sessions
  • [ ] OPENAI_API_KEY — only if using AI recommendation feature

docs.title: 4. Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] This documentation site (auto-generated from docs/)
  • [ ] Swagger API docs (auto-generated at /docs)

docs.title: 5. Knowledge Transfer
docs.text:
• [ ] How to add new interventions (edit DSS_input2.csv)
  • [ ] How to update shapefiles (replace files in data/)
  • [ ] How to add new AI documents (add PDFs to ai-docs/, call /api/ai-recommendation/init)
  • [ ] How to add GP data for new districts (new shapefile + Excel + mapping)

docs.title: Systemd Service (Linux)
docs.text:
Create /etc/systemd/system/iwmi-leaf.service:

[Unit]
Description=LEAF DSS Flask Application
After=network.target

[Service]
User=www-data
WorkingDirectory=/opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask
Environment="SECRET_KEY=your-secret-key-here"
Environment="OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-..."
ExecStart=/opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask/venv/bin/gunicorn app:app \
    --bind 127.0.0.1:5000 \
    --workers 2 \
    --timeout 120 \
    --access-logfile /var/log/iwmi-leaf/access.log \
    --error-logfile /var/log/iwmi-leaf/error.log
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

sudo mkdir -p /var/log/iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl enable iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl start iwmi-leaf
sudo systemctl status iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy
docs.text:
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name iwmi-leaf.yourdomain.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:5000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_read_timeout 120s;
    }

    # Optional: serve static files directly for better performance
    location /static/ {
        alias /opt/iwmi-leaf/leaf_flask/static/;
        expires 7d;
    }
}

Then enable HTTPS with Let's Encrypt:

sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx
sudo certbot --nginx -d iwmi-leaf.yourdomain.com

docs.title: Docker (Optional)
docs.text:
Create a Dockerfile in the project root:

FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies for GeoPandas
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libgdal-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY leaf_flask/requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt gunicorn

# Copy application
COPY leaf_flask/ ./leaf_flask/
COPY site/ ./site/
COPY ai-docs/ ./ai-docs/

WORKDIR /app/leaf_flask

EXPOSE 5000

CMD ["gunicorn", "app:app", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:5000", "--workers", "2", "--timeout", "120"]

docker build -t iwmi-leaf .
docker run -p 5000:5000 -e SECRET_KEY=mysecret iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Routine Updates
docs.text:
cd /opt/iwmi-leaf
git pull origin main
cd leaf_flask
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt   # Only if dependencies changed
cd .. && mkdocs build && cd leaf_flask  # Only if docs changed
sudo systemctl restart iwmi-leaf

docs.title: Updating Data
docs.text:
1. Replace the relevant files in leaf_flask/data/
  2. Restart the service — the LRU cache clears on restart
  3. No code changes needed

docs.title: Adding a New District's GP Data
docs.text:
1. Prepare a GP-level shapefile (.shp + sidecar files)
  2. Prepare a GP indicator Excel file (same column format as Tinsukia_GP_data.xlsx)
  3. Update data_utils.py to load the new files
  4. Update the gp_districts list in the /api/levels route
  5. Restart the service

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text: Area Current State Recommendation for Production Authentication None (open API) Add API key or OAuth if needed HTTPS Handled by Render/Nginx Always use HTTPS in production Secret key Default in code Set via environment variable CORS Open to all origins Restrict to your domain if needed Rate limiting None Add Flask-Limiter if public-facing Input validation Basic Sufficient for current use File uploads None No risk SQL injection N/A (no database) No risk
